meta.title: Unit Testing
meta.description: Jest, Vitest, pytest, JUnit, xUnit unit testing patterns and best practices

items.text: Do tests follow the Arrange-Act-Assert (AAA) pattern?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should have three clear phases: set up inputs (Arrange), call the code under test (Act), verify the result (Assert).
baseContent.whyItMatters: AAA makes tests readable and consistent. Readers instantly know what's being tested and what the expectation is.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that each test has a clear setup, a single action, and explicit assertions. Look for tests that mix these phases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mixes setup and assertions throughout. Restructure into clear Arrange/Act/Assert sections.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Follow AAA: set up, act, assert. One action per test.

items.text: Do test names describe the behavior being tested?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test names should describe what is being tested, under what conditions, and the expected outcome. Not 'test1' or 'testFunction'.
baseContent.whyItMatters: When a test fails, the name is the first thing you read. Good names tell you exactly what broke without reading the test body.
baseContent.howToVerify: Read test names without the test body. Can you tell what behavior is being verified? If not, the name needs improvement.
baseContent.exampleComment: 'testCalculate' doesn't describe the behavior. Use 'should return total with tax when items have taxable flag'.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Name tests by behavior: 'should [expected] when [condition]'. The name should explain the failure.

items.text: Does each test verify a single concept?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test should verify one behavior or concept. Multiple unrelated assertions in one test make it hard to diagnose failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Multi-assertion tests hide failures. If the first assertion fails, you never know if the others would have passed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests with many unrelated assertions. Related assertions (e.g., checking multiple properties of a result) are fine.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test checks creation, update, and deletion in one test. Split into three tests, each verifying one operation.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: One concept per test. Multiple related assertions are fine; multiple unrelated behaviors are not.

items.text: Are tests logically grouped (describe/context blocks)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Group related tests using describe/context blocks. Group by method, feature, or scenario for clear organization.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Grouped tests are easier to navigate. describe blocks provide context and allow shared setup via beforeEach.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that related tests are in the same describe block. Verify nesting isn't too deep (3 levels max).
baseContent.exampleComment: These 20 tests are all at the top level. Group by method: describe('calculate'), describe('validate'), etc.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Group tests with describe/context. Keep nesting shallow (2-3 levels max).

items.text: Are tests free of conditional logic (no if/else, loops, try/catch)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should be simple, linear code. No if/else, for loops, or try/catch. Each test should be a straight line from setup to assertion.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Logic in tests can silently skip assertions. A try/catch that swallows errors means the test always passes.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for if/else, for/while, and try/catch in test files. Each should be replaced with separate test cases.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test has a for loop over test cases. Use parameterized tests (test.each/pytest.mark.parametrize) instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: No logic in tests. Use parameterized tests for data-driven scenarios.

items.text: Is shared setup in beforeEach/setUp, not duplicated across tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Common setup code shared by multiple tests should be in beforeEach/setUp hooks. Test-specific setup stays in the test.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Duplicated setup is hard to maintain and makes tests verbose. Shared setup in hooks keeps tests focused on what's unique.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for identical setup code repeated across tests. This should be in beforeEach. Verify beforeEach isn't doing too much.
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test creates a new User with the same data. Move user creation to beforeEach.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Share common setup in hooks. Keep test-specific setup in the test.

items.text: Are mocks kept to a minimum (only external dependencies)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Only mock what you must: external services, databases, network calls, time. Don't mock the code under test or simple value objects.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-mocking tests the mocks, not the code. Tests pass but the real code is broken because mocks don't behave like the real thing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if mocks replace internal code that could be used directly. Verify each mock represents a genuine external dependency.
baseContent.exampleComment: This mocks the Calculator class to test OrderService. Use the real Calculator — it's fast and deterministic.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock boundaries (APIs, databases, clocks). Don't mock the code you're testing.

items.text: Are mock interactions verified (calls, arguments, call count)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When using mocks for side effects, verify the mock was called with correct arguments. Don't just check the return value.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A test that mocks an email service but never verifies send() was called doesn't actually test that emails are sent.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that mocked side effects (API calls, database writes, events) have corresponding verification assertions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This mocks the payment service but never verifies charge() was called. Add expect(paymentService.charge).toHaveBeenCalledWith(amount).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Verify mock interactions: what was called, with what arguments, how many times.

items.text: Are mocks properly reset between tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Reset or restore mocks in afterEach/tearDown. Leaked mocks from one test affect subsequent tests, causing flaky failures.
baseContent.whyItMatters: A mock from test A leaking into test B causes order-dependent failures. Tests pass individually but fail together.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for jest.restoreAllMocks(), mockReset(), or equivalent in afterEach. Verify module-level mocks are cleaned up.
baseContent.exampleComment: jest.spyOn(Date, 'now') is never restored. Add jest.restoreAllMocks() in afterEach to prevent leaking.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always clean up mocks. Use afterEach with restoreAllMocks() or equivalent.

items.text: Do mocks avoid coupling tests to implementation details?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Don't mock internal methods or verify private implementation. Test the public interface and observable behavior.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Tests coupled to implementation break on every refactor. You can't rename a private method without fixing dozens of tests.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if tests mock internal/private methods. Verify tests would still pass after an internal refactor.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test mocks the private _calculateDiscount method. Test the public getTotal() method instead and verify the final result.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test public behavior, not private implementation. Tests should survive refactoring.

items.text: Do test fixtures use realistic data (not 'foo', 'bar', 123)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use realistic test data that resembles production. Real names, valid emails, plausible amounts. Use factories or builders.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Placeholder data hides edge cases. 'foo@bar.com' won't catch email parsing bugs that 'user+tag@sub.domain.co.uk' would.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for 'test', 'foo', 'bar', '123' in test data. Verify test data represents realistic production scenarios.
baseContent.exampleComment: Using name: 'foo' and amount: 1 doesn't test real scenarios. Use realistic data: name: 'Jane Smith', amount: 49.99.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use realistic test data. Consider factories (fishery, factory_bot, AutoFixture) for consistency.

items.text: Are snapshot tests used appropriately (not as a lazy catch-all)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Snapshot tests capture output and compare to a stored file. They're good for UI components but bad for logic testing.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Large snapshots are never actually reviewed. Developers update them blindly, defeating their purpose. They catch changes, not bugs.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check snapshot size — large snapshots are red flags. Verify snapshots test visual output, not logic or data structures.
baseContent.exampleComment: This 500-line snapshot tests an API response. Write explicit assertions on the fields that matter instead.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use snapshots for UI output. Use explicit assertions for logic. Keep snapshots small and focused.

items.text: Are specific matchers used instead of generic assertTrue/toBeTruthy?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use toEqual, toContain, toThrow instead of toBeTruthy/assertTrue. Specific matchers give better error messages on failure.
baseContent.whyItMatters: assertTrue(result.includes('error')) says 'expected true, got false'. toContain('error') says 'expected array to contain error'.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for assertTrue/toBeTruthy with computed expressions. These should use specific matchers.
baseContent.exampleComment: expect(result.length > 0).toBe(true) gives a bad error message. Use expect(result).toHaveLength(expect.any(Number)) or similar.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use specific matchers. Better error messages mean faster debugging.

items.text: Are error cases and edge cases tested (not just happy paths)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test what happens with invalid input, empty data, null values, boundary conditions, and concurrent access.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Happy-path-only tests miss the most common bugs. Errors in error handling are the hardest to debug in production.
baseContent.howToVerify: For each function tested, verify there are tests for: empty input, null/undefined, boundary values, invalid types, error conditions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This only tests successful login. Add tests for: wrong password, locked account, expired token, missing fields.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test error paths as thoroughly as happy paths. Every if/else branch should have a test.

items.text: Are async tests properly awaited (no fire-and-forget)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Async tests must await promises or use done callbacks. A test without await on an async call passes before assertions run.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Un-awaited async tests always pass because assertions run after the test completes. The test is green but tests nothing.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for async operations without await. Verify promise rejections are caught with rejects/toThrow. Check for floating promises.
baseContent.exampleComment: This calls fetchUser() without await. The test passes before the assertion executes. Add await before fetchUser().
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Always await async operations in tests. Un-awaited tests silently pass without testing anything.

items.text: Are exceptions tested for type, message, and conditions?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When testing that code throws, verify the exception type and message. Don't just check that any exception was thrown.
baseContent.whyItMatters: expect(() => fn()).toThrow() passes for any error, including unexpected ones. Check the specific error you expect.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check exception tests for specific error types and messages. Verify they wouldn't pass on an unrelated error.
baseContent.exampleComment: toThrow() would pass even if the error is a TypeError from a typo. Use toThrow(new ValidationError('email required')).
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Assert on exception type and message. Generic toThrow() catches the wrong errors.

items.text: Are boundary values tested (0, -1, MAX_INT, empty string, null)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Test at the edges: 0, 1, -1, empty arrays, max values, min values. Off-by-one errors live at boundaries.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Most bugs occur at boundaries. A pagination function that works for page 5 might break at page 0 or the last page.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for boundary value tests. For numeric inputs, test 0, negative, max. For strings, test empty and very long.
baseContent.exampleComment: This tests pagination with page 1 and 5 but not page 0 or the last page. Add boundary tests.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Test boundaries: 0, empty, negative, max. Most bugs hide at the edges.

items.text: Does code coverage reflect meaningful tests (not just line coverage)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: High line coverage with weak assertions is useless. Tests should verify behavior, not just execute code paths.
baseContent.whyItMatters: 100% coverage with no assertions tests nothing. A test that calls a function without checking the result inflates coverage.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for tests that call functions without assertions. Verify assertions are meaningful, not just expect(result).toBeDefined().
baseContent.exampleComment: This test calls processOrder() but never checks the result. It adds coverage but tests nothing. Assert on the return value.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Measure assertion quality, not just line coverage. Every test needs meaningful assertions.

items.text: Are tests fully isolated (no shared mutable state)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Each test must create its own state. No module-level variables modified across tests. No test depending on another's output.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Shared state causes the most frustrating test failures. Tests pass individually, fail together, pass in different order.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for module-level let variables modified in tests. Verify tests don't depend on execution order.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests share a module-level 'users' array. Each test pushes to it, so later tests see accumulated data. Create fresh arrays per test.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Each test gets fresh state. No shared mutable variables between tests.

items.text: Do unit tests avoid real network calls, file I/O, and databases?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Unit tests should be fast and isolated. Mock all external I/O: HTTP requests, database queries, file system access, timers.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Real I/O makes tests slow, flaky, and environment-dependent. A test that calls a real API fails when the API is down.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for unmocked fetch/axios/http calls, database connections, or file system access in unit tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This unit test calls the real payment API. Mock the HTTP client to return test responses. Integration tests can use the real API.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unit tests mock all I/O. Save real calls for integration tests.

items.text: Are tests deterministic (no random data, no time-dependent assertions)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should produce the same result every time. Mock Date.now(), Math.random(), and other non-deterministic sources.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Non-deterministic tests are flaky. A test that fails on the 29th of February or at midnight is nearly impossible to debug.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for Date.now(), new Date(), Math.random() without mocking. Verify time-dependent assertions use fixed timestamps.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test asserts createdAt equals new Date(). If the test runs at a millisecond boundary, it fails. Use a fixed mock time.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Mock time and randomness. Tests must produce the same result every run.

items.text: Do tests run fast (under 10ms per test)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Unit tests should be extremely fast. If a test takes more than 10ms, it's probably doing real I/O or unnecessary computation.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Slow tests don't get run. A 30-second test suite gets run on every save. A 10-minute suite gets skipped or run only in CI.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check test execution times. Identify tests over 100ms. These likely need mocking or belong in integration tests.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test takes 500ms because it waits for a real timeout. Mock the timer or use fake timers.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Unit tests should be nearly instant. Slow tests indicate missing mocks.

items.text: Are there no arbitrary sleeps/delays in tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Never use setTimeout, sleep, or Thread.sleep in tests to wait for async operations. Use proper async patterns.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Sleep-based tests are slow and flaky. 100ms is too short on CI, too long for fast feedback. Use proper async waiting.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for sleep, setTimeout, Thread.sleep, time.sleep in test files. Replace with await, waitFor, or fake timers.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test sleeps 500ms waiting for a state update. Use waitFor() or act() to properly wait for the update.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Never sleep in tests. Use async utilities (waitFor, act, fake timers) instead.

items.text: Can tests run in parallel without interference?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should not interfere when run in parallel. No shared files, ports, database tables, or global state.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Parallel execution dramatically speeds up test suites. Tests that can't parallelize slow down CI and developer feedback.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check for hardcoded ports, shared temp files, or global singletons. Verify tests use unique identifiers for shared resources.
baseContent.exampleComment: Tests write to /tmp/test-output.json. When parallel, they overwrite each other. Use unique temp dirs per test.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Design tests for parallel execution. Use unique resources per test.

items.text: Do tests prioritize clarity over DRY (some duplication is OK)?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Tests should be readable in isolation. Some code duplication is acceptable if it makes each test self-explanatory.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Over-abstracted tests require jumping between helpers to understand. A developer should read one test and understand the full scenario.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check if test helpers obscure what's being tested. Verify each test can be understood without reading 5 other functions.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test calls setupScenario7() — what does that set up? Inline the relevant setup so the test is self-documenting.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Tests should be readable standalone. DRY is less important than clarity in tests.

items.text: Are test utilities (factories, builders, custom matchers) well-designed?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Use factories to create test objects with sensible defaults. Use custom matchers for domain-specific assertions.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Good test utilities reduce boilerplate and make tests more expressive. Bad utilities hide important details.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check that factories allow overriding relevant fields. Verify custom matchers provide clear error messages.
baseContent.exampleComment: Every test creates a User with 10 fields. Create a userFactory that provides defaults and allows overrides.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Build factories for test objects. Create custom matchers for domain assertions.

items.text: Are parameterized tests used for data-driven scenarios?

baseContent.whatItMeans: When multiple tests differ only in input/output data, use parameterized tests (test.each, @pytest.mark.parametrize, @ParameterizedTest).
baseContent.whyItMatters: Copy-pasted tests with different values are hard to maintain. Parameterized tests make it trivial to add new cases.
baseContent.howToVerify: Look for test methods that are identical except for values. These should use parameterized test features.
baseContent.exampleComment: These 5 tests only differ in the input currency. Use test.each to cover all currencies in one parameterized test.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Use parameterized tests for data-driven scenarios. One test template, many data points.

items.text: Are there no commented-out or skipped tests without explanation?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Commented-out tests and test.skip/xit without a reason indicate hidden failures. Fix, remove, or document why they're skipped.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Skipped tests rot. After a month, nobody remembers why they were skipped. They represent untested code.
baseContent.howToVerify: Search for xit, test.skip, @Ignore, @pytest.mark.skip, and commented-out test methods. Each needs a reason or should be removed.
baseContent.exampleComment: This test has been skipped for 6 months with no explanation. Either fix the underlying issue or remove the test.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: No unexplained skipped tests. Fix them, delete them, or add a comment explaining why.

items.text: Are new code paths covered by corresponding tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every new function, branch, or error path should have a corresponding test. New code without tests is a regression waiting to happen.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Untested code changes are the primary source of production bugs. Tests catch regressions before they reach users.
baseContent.howToVerify: Compare new/changed code with new/changed tests. Every new if/else branch should have a test for both paths.
baseContent.exampleComment: This PR adds a new discount calculation but no tests. Add tests for: no discount, percentage discount, fixed discount, and max cap.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: New code needs new tests. No exceptions for 'simple' code — simple code has bugs too.

items.text: Are bug fixes accompanied by regression tests?

baseContent.whatItMeans: Every bug fix should include a test that fails without the fix and passes with it. This prevents the bug from recurring.
baseContent.whyItMatters: Without a regression test, the same bug can be reintroduced by future changes. The test is proof the bug is fixed.
baseContent.howToVerify: Check bug fix PRs for accompanying tests. Verify the test would fail if the fix were reverted.
baseContent.exampleComment: This fixes a null pointer but adds no test. Add a test with the null input that triggered the bug.
baseContent.keyTakeaway: Every bug fix gets a regression test. The test should fail without the fix.
